heroText: Male is a capital city in Maldives, connecting local landmarks, event context, transport access and seasonal reasons to plan a visit.

highlights.name: Male
highlights.text: Male is a capital city in Maldives, connecting local landmarks, event context, transport access and seasonal reasons to plan a visit.

history.label: Origins
history.text: The whole island group, the Maldives, is named after its capital.

When Ibn Battuta traveled to Malé in 1343 (in Arabic: المَحَل, romanized: al-Maḥal thus the entire Maldivian islands were ذِيبَةُ المَحَل Dhībat-ul-Maḥal), he provided a rather extensive description of the city as well as.

history.label: 17th century
history.text: Malé was fortified in the 17th century by the sultan Muhammad Imaduddin, who built walls on the north, east, and west sides of the island.
The old forts and decrepit walls were dismantled in 1925–1927 under the reign of Muhammad Shamsuddeen III, to be rebuilt on a smaller scale.
Malé's residents soon grew to 11,453 by 1967 and 29,522 by 1977.

history.label: Modern city
history.text: The first settlers in the Maldivian islands were Dravidian people who arrived from the neighboring shores of the modern Indian subcontinent and coastal Ceylon.

history.label: Today
history.text: Comparative studies of Maldivian linguistic, oral, and other cultural traditions, in addition to folklore, point to a strong Dravidian influence on Maldivian society, centered in Malé, from ancient times.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Early records also indicate that the island was called "Athamana Huraa" before being named Malé.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: According to regional lore, Giraavaru fishermen used to go regularly to a certain large sandbank (finolhu) at the southern end of their atoll to clean tuna fish after a good catch.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: A palace was built and the island was formally named Maa-le (Malé), while the nearest island was named Hulhu-le.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: He mentioned that the Queen, Rehendhi Khadeeja, had a residence in Malé, which from its description may be assimilated to the same palace of the later sultan rulers, in the centre of the island.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Within the palace compounds, several pits contained stores of cowrie shells, ready to be traded.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The Royal Palace (Gan'duvaru) was destroyed along with the picturesque forts (koshi) and bastions (buruzu) when the city was remodelled under President Ibrahim Nasir's rule in the aftermath of the abolition of the monarchy.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Only the National Museum building, the residence of the last sultan, as well as the Malé Friday Mosque, remain.
